key_insight: Atmospheric plasma-sprayed metal–graphite coatings on titanium bipolar plates improve both corrosion resistance and interfacial contact resistance, with higher graphite content giving better overall performance.
engineering_relevance: This offers a practical coating route to make lightweight titanium bipolar plates more viable for durable, low-resistance PEMFC operation.
method: Prepared Ni–graphite and Cu–graphite coatings on titanium plates by atmospheric plasma spraying (APS)
Varied graphite content to study its effect on phase, microstructure, corrosion behavior, and conductivity
Evaluated performance using potentiostatic corrosion testing and interfacial contact resistance (ICR) measurements
evidence_signal: The best reported coatings reached potentiostatic corrosion current densities of 1.05 × 10^-4 A·cm^-2 (Ni–G) and 0.91 × 10^-4 A·cm^-2 (Cu–G), with ICR as low as 25.2 mΩ·cm^-2 for Ni–G and 17.4 mΩ·cm^-2 for Cu–G before cathodic polarization.
watch_item: The abstract does not report long-term cycling, full-cell PEMFC validation, or adhesion/wear durability, so real operating stability still needs confirmation.
paper_title: Investigation of Metal-Graphite Coatings for PEMFC Bipolar Plate Using Atmospheric Plasma Thermal Spraying
